question: Which three channels in the 2.4 GHz band are considered non-overlapping in North America and are typically used to minimize co-channel interference?
feedback: In 2.4 GHz, channels 1, 6, and 11 are spaced far enough apart (with 20 MHz channel width) that their spectra do not overlap. Using other combinations causes adjacent-channel interference.

question: Which of the following statements about Wi-Fi frequency bands are accurate?
correct: 2.4 GHz penetrates walls better but has fewer clear channels
5 GHz offers more channels and usually higher throughput than 2.4 GHz
6 GHz offers more spectrum but usually shorter range than 5 GHz
Higher-frequency bands generally lose strength faster through obstacles
incorrect: The 2.4 GHz band has more non-overlapping channels than 5 GHz in normal deployments
The 6 GHz band is part of the 2.4 GHz range and uses the same channel plan
All Wi-Fi clients can use 6 GHz regardless of hardware generation
feedback: Lower frequencies propagate better; higher frequencies offer more spectrum and speed. Only Wi-Fi 6E and newer clients can use 6 GHz.

question: Which feature lets a dual-band access point encourage capable clients to connect to the less congested 5 GHz radio instead of 2.4 GHz?
correct: Band steering
feedback: Band steering pushes capable dual-band clients toward 5 GHz (or 6 GHz), leaving 2.4 GHz for legacy devices and improving overall performance.

question: In the 5 GHz band, which standard provides Dynamic Frequency Selection (DFS) and Transmit Power Control (TPC) so Wi-Fi can coexist with radar and satellite services?
feedback: 802.11h adds DFS and TPC in the 5 GHz band to meet regulatory requirements for sharing spectrum with radar. 802.11i is security (WPA2); 802.11ax is Wi-Fi 6.

question: Which of the following correctly describes a BSSID?
correct: The MAC address of an access point's radio identifying a single Basic Service Set
incorrect: The human-readable network name a user selects when joining Wi-Fi
A group of multiple APs sharing the same SSID across a large area
A Layer 3 IP address used by the wireless LAN controller
feedback: An SSID is the human-readable name. A BSSID is the MAC address identifying one AP's radio. Multiple APs sharing the same SSID form an ESS, and each has its own BSSID.

question: At Ravenshade Academy, many APs across several buildings share the same network name (SSID) so that clients can roam seamlessly. What is this arrangement called?
correct: Extended Service Set (ESS)
feedback: An ESS is multiple BSSs that share the same SSID and are connected by a common distribution system, enabling roaming across APs.

question: Which wireless network type is formed when two laptops connect directly to each other with no access point?
correct: Ad hoc
feedback: Ad hoc (IBSS) networks are peer-to-peer. Infrastructure networks use an AP. Mesh networks have APs that backhaul to each other wirelessly.

question: Old Hask needs to connect two buildings about 400 meters apart wirelessly. Which antenna type is most appropriate for the outdoor link on each building?
correct: High-gain directional antenna (such as a Yagi or parabolic)
incorrect: Omnidirectional whip antenna on each end
feedback: Point-to-point outdoor links benefit from directional antennas that focus RF energy toward the far end, increasing effective range and reducing interference.

question: Which of the following statements about omnidirectional vs. directional antennas are accurate?
correct: Omnidirectional antennas radiate RF roughly equally in a horizontal plane and are common on indoor APs
Directional antennas concentrate RF energy in a specific direction and offer higher gain along that axis
Directional antennas are well suited to point-to-point outdoor links
incorrect: Omnidirectional antennas radiate equally in all directions in a perfect sphere
Directional antennas are ideal for covering a single open-plan office from the center of the ceiling
Directional and omnidirectional antennas are interchangeable with no impact on coverage
feedback: Omnidirectional = broad coverage, lower gain. Directional = focused coverage, higher gain. Choosing the wrong one creates dead zones or unwanted spillover.

question: Which of the following are differences between WPA2 and WPA3?
correct: WPA3 uses SAE instead of WPA2's PSK handshake to resist offline guessing
WPA3 requires Protected Management Frames to reduce deauthentication attacks
WPA3-Enterprise supports stronger 192-bit suites than WPA2-Enterprise
incorrect: WPA2 encrypts traffic, but WPA3 does not encrypt user traffic
WPA3 removed the option for enterprise 802.1X authentication entirely
feedback: WPA3 mainly improves the authentication handshake (SAE instead of PSK exchange) and mandates PMF. Both WPA2 and WPA3 encrypt traffic and support personal (PSK) and enterprise (802.1X) modes.

question: Aria is deploying enterprise Wi-Fi at Silverkeep. She wants each user authenticated against Active Directory with unique credentials rather than everyone sharing a passphrase. Which authentication mode should she use?
correct: WPA2/WPA3-Enterprise with 802.1X and RADIUS
incorrect: Open authentication with a captive portal only
feedback: Enterprise mode uses 802.1X/EAP with a RADIUS backend, giving each user individual credentials and per-session keys. PSK is simpler but shared among everyone.

question: The Gilded Tankard wants guests to accept a terms-of-service page and enter an email before using its free Wi-Fi. Which feature provides this?
correct: Captive portal
feedback: A captive portal intercepts HTTP traffic from unauthenticated guest clients and redirects them to an acceptance or login page before granting internet access.

question: Which of the following are true about autonomous (fat) versus lightweight (thin) access points?
correct: Autonomous APs are individually configured and run their own full software stack
Lightweight APs rely on a wireless LAN controller for configuration and forwarding decisions
Lightweight APs are better suited to large deployments where centralized management is important
incorrect: Autonomous APs require a wireless LAN controller to forward any traffic
Lightweight APs always operate with no controller involved
Autonomous APs scale more easily than lightweight APs for thousand-AP deployments
feedback: Autonomous APs are self-contained and fine for small deployments. Lightweight APs offload control to a WLC, which simplifies mass deployment, firmware updates, and roaming.

question: The human-readable network name broadcast by an access point that users see when choosing a Wi-Fi network is called the ___.
correct: SSID
Service Set Identifier
service set identifier
feedback: The SSID is the advertised network name. It can be shared across many APs (an ESS) to allow roaming, while each AP still has its own BSSID.

question: Wider channels provide more bandwidth, but in 2.4 GHz, using a 40 MHz-wide channel causes significant ___ with neighboring networks.
correct: interference
overlap
feedback: The 2.4 GHz band is too narrow to fit multiple 40 MHz channels without overlap. Wider channels are practical in 5 GHz and 6 GHz, where there is more spectrum.

question: The Wi-Fi encryption standard introduced as the successor to WPA2, which uses SAE instead of a PSK handshake, is ___.
correct: WPA3
feedback: WPA3 replaces WPA2's shared-secret handshake with SAE, providing forward secrecy and resistance to offline password guessing.

question: Match each wireless network type to its description.

pairs.definition: Clients connect through one or more access points to a wired network
Peer-to-peer network formed directly between wireless devices with no access point
Access points form wireless backhaul links among themselves to extend coverage
A dedicated wireless link between exactly two endpoints, often using directional antennas

feedback: These topology types differ in whether an AP is involved and how APs relate to each other.

question: Where is the primary demarcation and aggregation point for a building's structured cabling, typically housing the carrier handoff, core switches, and main cross-connects, usually located?
correct: Main Distribution Frame (MDF)
feedback: The MDF is the central wiring facility for a building. IDFs are satellite wiring closets on individual floors or wings that home-run back to the MDF.

question: Which of the following are true about IDF and MDF placement?
correct: Each IDF typically serves a floor or wing and connects back to the MDF via a riser uplink
Copper horizontal runs are limited to about 100 meters, which drives how many IDFs a building needs
Both MDFs and IDFs should be lockable and environmentally controlled
incorrect: There is no practical distance limit on copper horizontal cabling, so one MDF can serve any building
IDFs should be placed in unsecured common areas for convenience
The MDF and IDF must use different VLAN numbering schemes
feedback: The 100-meter Ethernet limit drives the need for IDFs in large buildings. Both facilities should be physically secured and climate-controlled.

question: Equipment racks in modern data centers are most commonly sized using which standard unit of vertical height?
correct: Rack unit (U), equal to 1.75 inches (44.45 mm)
incorrect: Millimeter height only, with no standardized unit
feedback: A rack unit (U or RU) is 1.75 inches. Standard racks are 42U or 45U tall and 19 inches wide.

question: In a data center with hot-aisle/cold-aisle containment, how should switches with port-side exhaust airflow be oriented?
correct: With the ports (and exhaust) facing the hot aisle, so cool air is drawn in from the cold aisle
incorrect: With the ports facing the cold aisle to push hot air at the users
With the ports facing either direction — airflow does not matter for switches
With the ports facing up to vent through the ceiling
feedback: Switches come in port-side-intake or port-side-exhaust variants. Choosing and orienting them correctly keeps the cold aisle cold and avoids recirculating hot exhaust.

question: Which passive component terminates horizontal cable runs from wall jacks at the wiring closet and presents them as RJ45 jacks for patching into switches?
correct: Patch panel
feedback: A patch panel organizes permanent horizontal runs and presents them as front-facing RJ45 jacks. Short patch cables then connect each jack to a switch port.

question: A small rack holds a core switch, a firewall, and a server, and must continue operating for a few minutes during a power hit long enough to shut down cleanly or let the generator start. Which device provides that short-term battery backup?
correct: Uninterruptible Power Supply (UPS)
feedback: A UPS provides battery-backed power for minutes, long enough to ride through short outages or trigger an orderly shutdown / generator start. A PDU distributes power but does not store energy.

question: Which of the following are true about Power Distribution Units (PDUs) in equipment racks?
correct: They distribute power from a single feed to multiple outlets inside the rack
Managed PDUs can monitor per-outlet current and support remote on/off
Racks with dual power supplies are commonly fed by two PDUs on separate circuits for redundancy
incorrect: A PDU provides battery backup during power outages
A PDU is functionally identical to a UPS
Rack PDUs must only ever be fed from a single circuit to prevent redundancy
feedback: PDUs distribute power and can be smart/managed, but they do not store energy. True redundancy comes from dual PSUs fed by separate PDUs on separate circuits, with UPS backup upstream.

question: Which of the following environmental factors should be controlled in an MDF or data center room?
correct: Temperature, typically kept within ranges like ASHRAE's recommended envelope
Humidity, to avoid condensation when too high and static discharge when too low
Appropriate fire suppression, often using clean-agent systems rather than water
incorrect: Direct sunlight, which should be maximized to reduce lighting costs
Dust accumulation, which should be encouraged to insulate equipment
Open floor drains in the middle of the room, to simplify cleaning
feedback: Data centers need controlled temperature, humidity, and non-water fire suppression (e.g., FM-200, Novec 1230) to protect electronics.

question: A battery-backed device that provides short-term power during outages and typically also filters line power is called a ___.
correct: UPS
Uninterruptible Power Supply
uninterruptible power supply
feedback: A UPS bridges brief outages and smooths out power events. Longer outages are handled by a generator, with the UPS providing ride-through while the generator starts.

question: A clean-agent fire suppression system is preferred over water sprinklers in data centers because water would ___ the electronics.
correct: damage
destroy
ruin
short
feedback: Water and electronics don't mix. Clean-agent systems (FM-200, Novec 1230, inert gas) suppress fire without damaging powered equipment.

question: Match each physical-installation component to its purpose.

pairs.definition: Central wiring facility where carrier demarcation and core equipment live
Satellite wiring closet that serves a floor or wing and homes back to the MDF
Passive termination for horizontal copper runs, exposed as RJ45 jacks
Battery-backed power source that rides through short outages
Distributes power from a feed to multiple outlets within a rack

feedback: These physical components form the backbone of any structured-cabling and power design.

question: Which of the following channel widths is commonly used in the 2.4 GHz band to maintain three non-overlapping channels in a typical indoor deployment?
correct: 20 MHz
feedback: 20 MHz channels preserve the 1/6/11 non-overlapping plan in 2.4 GHz. Wider 40/80/160 MHz channels are practical in 5 GHz and 6 GHz, where there is much more spectrum.

question: Lyra wants to physically protect network equipment from tampering by unauthorized staff. Which of the following are reasonable physical-security measures for an IDF?
correct: Install the IDF in a dedicated, lockable room or cabinet
Record and log entry using a card reader or keypad
Keep patch cables tidy so unauthorized additions are easier to spot
incorrect: Leave the IDF door propped open for airflow
Mount the IDF in a public hallway with no enclosure
Label each switch port with the password of the admin account
feedback: Physical security is a fundamental control: lockable enclosures, logged entry, and tidy cable management all reduce the risk of unauthorized access or tampering.
